steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks how Text 2's author explains why Beaulieu and Dufour's moose study found no predation effect while other predation relief studies do show prey increases. The key must come from Text 2's own argument.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They may be partially attributable to physical characteristics of ash-grey mice that are not shared by moose.: Text 2 explicitly argues that other studies use small, rapidly reproducing prey (like mice), while moose are large and slow-reproducing — that difference drives the divergent results. ✓
  • Choice B — They may have been different if the ratio of the body size of feral cats to that of ash-grey mice was greater than it is.: Text 2 does not discuss predator size ratios; this is introduced nowhere in the passage. ✗
  • Choice C — They may be partially explained by the fact that feral cats are smaller and reproduce more rapidly than wolves.: Text 2 does not compare feral cats to wolves; this comparison is absent. ✗
  • Choice D — They may reflect a response that does not tend to be observed in prey species other than ash-grey mice.: Text 2 explicitly states that many predation relief studies show similar increases in various small prey, so D contradicts the passage. ✗
A is correct.
